text:
RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TB1-17512
TB1-17538 TB1-17539 TB1-17540
TB1-17541

Administrative De Novo
Reasons and Decision ? Motifs et Décision

XXXX XXXX XXXX
XXXX XXXX XXXX
XXXX XXXX XXXX
XXXX XXXX XXXX
XXXX XXXX

Date(s) of Hearing
December 9, 2014
Date(s) de l'audience
Place of Hearing
Toronto, Ontario
Lieu de l'audience
Date of Decision
May 20, 2015
Date de la décision
and reasons
et des motifs
Panel
Edward C. Robinson
Tribunal
Counsel for the Claimant(s)
Christina M. Gural
Barrister and Solicitor
Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Designated Representative(s)
XXXX XXXX
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)
Counsel for the Minister
Catherine Bond
Conseil du (de la) ministre
[1] The claimant XXXX XXXX (hereafter the claimant), and her children, XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX are citizens of Croatia, and are claiming refugee protection pursuant to sections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.1 The claimant was made Designated Representative for her minor dependants, XXXX and XXXX.
ALLEGATIONS
[2] The claimant's allegations are set out in her Personal Information Form (PIF).2 She also provided additional information through testimony at the oral hearing. She alleged that she is a 53 year old woman who was born in XXXX, Serbia. She alleged that her parents moved to Serbia when she was four years old. The claimant returned to Serbia, was married, and lived in XXXX XXXX for eighteen years. She has two children from that union, XXXX and XXXX. The claimant alleged that the marriage was abusive. In 1992, she moved with her children to XXXX, Croatia. The claimant alleged that she met XXXX XXXX. In XXXX of that same year, they began a common-law relationship. The union produced four children. The claimant alleged that she was considered a foreign citizen in Croatia because her parents did not register her as a Croatian citizen and she had to renounce her Serbian citizenship in order to marry XXXX XXXX. She alleged that she married him on XXXX XXXX, 2000. As a result, she only has Croatian citizenship.
[3] The claimant alleged that things were good between her and her husband until he began drinking excessively. Consequently, around 2006, the larger problems began. She alleged he became very abusive and accused her of being unfaithful. She alleged that he would beat their children. The claimant also alleged that on many occasions she called the police, but the police did not care much because she and her family were just worthless Roma. She alleged that, in 2010, her husband was arrested as a result of his behaviour towards his family. Upon his release, he was issued a restraining order which prohibited him from coming within 300 meters of their residence. The claimant alleged that her husband however moved to their neighbour's home, which was located about 30 meters from their family home, and continued to harass her regularly. She alleged that she reported the breach to the authorities and he was fined 2,700 Kunas. However, she alleged that he was not compelled to leave the area.
[4] The claimant alleged that in 2011, XXXX, her daughter, visited her. The claimant alleges that her husband raped her daughter. She alleged that he was arrested, but later released with only a fine. The claimant then moved out with her children and went to live at XXXX home in XXXX XXXX, but her husband came there and attacked her and bit her on one of her ears. The police were called and he was arrested. The claimant was treated at hospital, but she alleged that on XXXX XXXX, she moved back to her home in XXXX. The claimant however alleged that she filed for divorce; the divorce was finalized on XXXX XXXX, 2010. But, her husband was still coming to the home and beating her.
[5] The claimant alleged that one day she returned home and found her ex-husband on top of XXXX while their other daughter, XXXX was screaming. She called the police, but was told by the police that they were fed up with their Gypsy matters.
[6] The claimant alleged that on XXXX 2011 her ex-husband was arrested and he spent three months in jail, but on his release he returned to harassing and beating the claimant. He was arrested again and held until XXXX XXXX, 2011. The claimant alleged that while he was in prison she and her children left Croatia and came to Canada. On October 28, 2011 the claimant and her children filed for refugee status.
DETERMINATION
[7] The panel finds that the claimant has not established that there is a serious possibility of persecution on a Convention ground, or that, on a balance of probabilities she would personally be subjected to a danger of torture, or face a risk to life, or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment, or punishment upon return to her country. The panel's reasons are as follows.
[8] The determinative issues in this case are credibility and state protection.
ANALYSIS
Identity
[9] The panel is satisfied that the claimants are citizens of Croatia based on the claimant's testimony and the supporting documentation filed including her personal identity based on certified true copies of their Croatian passports.
[10] The Chairperson's Gender Guidelines3 were taken into account when considering the process of the hearing and the facts in this case. All relevant factors, such as the social and cultural context in which the claimant found herself, were considered pursuant to the Chairperson's Gender Guidelines. Given the sensitive nature of the allegations, the panel is cognizant of the difficulties faced by the claimant in establishing her claim including cultural factors, the milieu of the hearing room, and the stress inherent in responding to oral questions through an interpreter. The panel has taken these considerations into account in arriving at its finding of negative credibility. The panel addressed the claimant with heightened sensitivity and avoided unnecessary detail when asking questions about sensitive issues.
State Protection and Credibility
[11] For the following reasons, the panel finds that adequate state protection exists for individuals like the claimant in Croatia. Based on the totality of evidence adduced, the panel finds that Croatian authorities are making serious efforts to provide adequate protection at the operational level to individuals like the claimant. The panel finds that the claimant has not met the burden of establishing clear and convincing evidence of lack of state protection.
[12] The panel has considered the evidence including the claimant's testimony, PIF narratives, counsel's submissions, and the documentary evidence. A claimant who alleges that state protection is inadequate must persuade the Board, on a balance of probabilities, that the evidence establishes that state protection is inadequate.4 To rebut the presumption of state protection the claimant must provide clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect. The evidence that state protection is inadequate, must not only be reliable and probative, it must also satisfy the Board, on a balance of probabilities, that state protection is inadequate.5 Unfortunately, no government is expected to guarantee perfect protection to all its citizens at all times, and the fact that a state is not always successful in protecting its citizens is not enough to justify a claim, especially where a state has control of its territories, has military, police, and civil authorities in place and is making a serious effort to protect its citizens.6
[13] The courts have found that states are presumed to be capable of protecting their citizens, except in the case of state that is in complete breakdown.7 Croatia is a state which entered the European Union, and as such had to address its human rights conditions, its judiciary, its security, and all aspects of its governance to meet the standards of the European Union. The presumption then is that the state is capable of protecting its citizens. Having reviewed the National Documentation Package and documents from the claimant, the panel finds that Croatia is a democracy in control of its territory and has a functioning security apparatus to uphold its laws and its constitution.8
[14] The Republic of Croatia is a constitutional parliamentary democracy. Legislative authority is vested in the unicameral parliament (the Sabor). The president serves as head of state and nominates the prime minister, who leads the government. Domestic and international observers stated that parliamentary elections held in December were in accordance with international standards. Security forces reported to civilian authorities.9
[15] The panel finds that Croatia has a functioning security apparatus and judicial processes, which have removed barriers to accessing assistance from the legal community.
"The national police have primary responsibility for domestic national security. However, in times of disorder, the prime minister and the president may call upon the military to provide security. The intelligence service is under the authority of the prime minister and the president. An independent oversight board monitors the intelligence service's activity."
"The constitution and law provide for the right to a public trial, and an independent judiciary generally enforced this right. Defendants enjoy the presumption of innocence. To hear cases, the legal system uses panels of judges that in some cases include lay judges, rather than juries. Defendants have the right to counsel, to be present at trial, to confront or question witnesses against them, and to present witnesses and evidence on their behalf. Defendants have access to evidence relevant to their cases and have the right to appeal."
"On April 6, the Constitutional Court annulled five articles of the law on free legal aid due to restrictive and vague provisions that hindered vulnerable individuals from accessing free legal aid. On July 14, the amended law addressing the Constitutional Court's requirements took effect. However, a group of 30 NGOs, including the umbrella NGO Human Rights House, the ombudsman, and legal experts from the Zagreb Law School, doubted that the new legislation would significantly improve the referral system or make legal aid more accessible."10
[16] When the state in question is a democratic state, the way the burden of proof rests on the claimant is directly proportional to the level of the democracy of the state in question; the more democratic the state's institutions, the more the claimant must have done to exhaust all courses of action open to him or her.11 Refugee protection is meant to be a form of surrogate protection to be invoked only in those situations where the refugee claimant has unsuccessfully sought the protection of their home state. The onus is on the claimant to approach the state for protection in situations where state protection might be reasonably forthcoming. To qualify for refugee status, a claimant must satisfy the Board that she has sought, but was unable to obtain, protection from her home state, or alternatively, that her home state, on an objective basis, could not be expected to provide protection. In the absence of a compelling explanation, a failure to pursue state protection opportunities within the claimant's home state will usually be fatal to a refugee claim, especially where the state is a functioning democracy with willingness and the apparatus necessary to provide a measure of protection to its citizens.
The panel is guided by the Federal Court of Appeal, which has ruled that testimony given under oath is presumed to be true, unless there is a valid reason to doubt its truthfulness.12 The panel does not believe that the claimant was a victim of persecution in her country because she was victimized by her husband since 2006 and despite making complaints to the authorities received no protection. The panel finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the claimant has used a basic set of facts, specifically that domestic violence is a problem in Croatia and that Roma face widespread discrimination, and through manipulation of those facts, the claimant has concocted a story that she did not receive adequate state protection in her country. The claimant stated that on many occasions she called the police, but they did not care much. However, there is no persuasive evidence before this panel that the claimant called the police on 'many occasions' between 2006 and 2010 and that they were indifferent to her calls. Counsel in his submissions stated that the claimant "provided evidence of past incidents in which state protection failed to materialize in spite of efforts to engage such protection".13 He also added that "Ms. XXXX explained how police refused to assist her when she attempted to obtain help in regard to domestic violence, and that, instead of being offered protection, she was at times called derogatory names linked to her Roma ethnicity".14 In addition, counsel in his written submissions cited several pieces of documentary evidence about police indifference to complaints made both by victims of domestic violence and instances of discrimination against Roma in order to suggest that the claimant's story of a lack of state protection is credible and should be believed.
[17] The panel disagrees with counsel that the claimant did not obtain adequate state protection and in doing so reminds itself that it is not sufficient to rely solely on documentary evidence listing certain flaws or weaknesses in the country's justice system.15 As the panel noted before, there is no persuasive evidence before it, that the claimant was a victim of domestic violence prior to 2010. Counsel makes the point, speculatively as it may be, that "Although Ms. XXXX was able to attain police involvement in her situation in 2010 and 2011, it appears that it was only because of the involvement of a social services agency that the police decided to address the matter in 2010. For many years prior to 2010 Ms. XXXX plea for assistance from the police were ignored"16 . If the claimant was a victim of domestic violence prior to 2010 and had sought help from the police, as the claimant said she did, on a balance of probabilities, she would have received help in much the same way she received help in 2010 and after. For example, there is no evidence before this panel that the social services agency which counsel alluded to in his submissions only came into existence in 2010. Or that prior to 2010 there was not any help available for victims of domestic violence. The panel notes that "In 2003, the Government adopted the Law on Protection against Domestic Violence (LPDV)".17 Centers for Social Welfare (CSWs), which counsel alluded to "as social services agency" are mandated to offer free counselling services, among a variety of other services.18 Their role is to protect and prevent domestic violence. CSWs are mandated under the LPDV and the Family Law to offer a wide range of measures to victims. Such services may include referrals to shelters, awarding victims one-off financial assistance, requiring victims, perpetrators and children to attend treatment programmes, making recommendations to the courts for perpetrator punishment, and making recommendations for the custody of children. In addition, CSWs must comply with mandatory reporting requirements and conduct investigations. They also act as gatekeepers between victims and shelter, and even if they have gone to the police, victims are required to register with the CSW to be placed in publicly-run or -funded shelters19. Counsel is on record for acknowledging that a CSW got involved.
[18] The panel believes that on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010 the claimant's husband and his nephew, XXXX XXXX were involved in a physical and verbal altercation for which they were both charged by the police. Later that evening the claimant was also physically and verbally assaulted by her husband. The claimant's husband also verbally assaulted their daughter XXXX. As a consequence, the claimant's husband was arrested, charged, tried, convicted in a court of law on four counts, and sentenced (1st count) 15 days, (2nd count) fined 730.00 kuna, (3rd count) 30 days, and (4th count) 45 days, for a total of 90 days imprisonment. As the panel understands the court's findings, he was to serve 90 days minus time served or15 days (credited for time served) and ordered removed from the residence for 3 months.20 The panel believes this was the first instance of physical violence between the claimant and her husband. But the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant and her husband had engaged in heated arguments in the past. For example, as the court record indicated, the children "got frightened due to the fact that this was not the first time that they argued between them".21 This suggests to the panel that the claimant and her husband would argue before the children.
[19] As the panel notes, there is no mention of any previous physical violence and finds that if there was any previous instance of physical violence, on a balance of probabilities it would have been mentioned just like how it was mentioned that it was not the first time they argued between themselves. This incident of physical violence, as it should be, was enough for the claimant. Divorce proceedings were commenced. She wrote "I had filed for divorce in the year 2010, which was finalized on XXXX XXXX, 2010".22 The panel finds on a balance of probabilities that because physical violence, for the first time, was now introduced into the equation in XXXX 2010, the incident was reported to the authorities, and action was taken. In addition, because the violence was now reported and the divorce proceedings had commenced, and especially since children were also involved, the CSW also got involved in accordance with the LPDV. For example, according to documentary evidence, The Family Law requires married couples (and their children) going through divorce proceedings to complete mediation as part of the divorce process.23 Thus, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the police did not get involved in the case of domestic violence merely because the CSW was involved. In fact, they were involved before the CSW. For example, the CSW report indicate "starting from XXXX XXXX XXXX.2010 to XXXX XXXX XXXX.2012",24 while the judgment of the court became effective on XXXX XXXX, 2010. At any rate, the important thing is that the police, when the matter came to their attention, took action. The panel however notes that the claimant advised the CSW that her husband "several years back, almost on a daily basis threatens conducts toward his wife and children psychological and physical mistreatment, consumes alcohol and then utters towards wife and children physical threats".25
[20] Counsel, however, admitted that the claimant received protection, even if she did so reluctantly. For example, she submitted that "police provided some assistance" to Ms. XXXX which led to her abusive spouse's charges, conviction, and incarceration. But she added that because of her (a) past experience and efforts to seek state protection on many occasions did not materialize, and the fact that the (2) police failed to enforce court orders and the (3) country condition documents, state protection is rebutted. Again the panel disagrees. There is no reliable evidence before this panel that the claimant made any complaints to the authorities prior to 2010. Counsel pointed to the claimant's testimony, she "testified how her former spouse routinely violated Court orders which imposed no-contact provisions"26 and that the "police failed to act to enforce the Court Orders or charge her former spouse upon breaches, although Ms. XXXX reported the breaches to the police"27 . According to counsel, because state protection was not provided when the claimant requested it earlier (before 2010) the presumption of state protection is therefore rebutted. But the claimant's allegation that her husband breached court orders and that the police failed to respond to her complaints is not supported by the documentary evidence she provided. The panel prefers the documentary evidence over the claimant's testimony since they are drawn from a wide range of publicly accessible documents from reliable non-government and government organizations.
[21] The panel notes that the CSW report asks that the claimant and her children be encouraged to seek protection from the police and inform the CSW regarding repeated violence by XXXX XXXX.28 This means that the CSW knew that state protection was available. The evidence before this panel clearly indicates that the claimants received adequate state protection. This protection was at the operational level. The report was made and the authorities saw it through to the end.
[22] To that extent on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2011 the claimant's ex-husband, by now the couple was divorced, but were living in a common-law relationship had physically and verbally assaulted the claimant. The panel understands that victims of domestic violence sometimes forgive their partners, reconcile, and live together again. Notwithstanding, the incident was reported, as it should have been, and he was arrested and charged, as he should have been. The panel notes that he was brought before the court on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2011. He pled guilty and was sentenced to 90 days imprisonment. In the court's judgment against the claimant's husband, they took into account his previous convictions and the fact that he was a repeat offender. They also found no mitigating factor to be lenient in any way.29 Therefore, there is no reliable evidence before this panel that the authorities did not provide the claimant and her children with adequate protection. Furthermore, there is no reliable evidence before this panel that the claimant's husband breached any protection order, which she allegedly reported and about which the police did nothing. This appears to have been concocted by the claimant. The panel notes that the protection order for the XXXX 2010 incident had expired and that the claimant and her ex-husband had resumed living together.
[23] For example, the court report states that Mr. XXXX "still resides with the claimant in a common-law union although they were married to one another before and divorced each other".30 This is apparent in the court document. If Mr. XXXX had breached his restraining order, on a balance of probabilities it would have been mentioned. There is no reliable evidence that Mr. XXXX, at any time, had breached any restraining order. The claimant herself testified that as soon as the restraining order had expired "he was back with us". Thus, the panel finds on a balance of probabilities that the claimant made up the story that her counsel so eloquently echoed that "her former spouse routinely violated Court orders which imposed no-contact provisions"31 and that the "police failed to act to enforce the Court Orders or charge her former spouse upon breaches, although Ms. XXXX reported the breaches to the police".32 The claimant did so to bolster her claim for refugee status. Even if the claimant thought that the court should have done more for her. The panel reminds itself that no state can guarantee perfect protection.33,34,35
Rape
[24] The claimant testified that her ex-husband raped her daughter from her first marriage and subsequently tried to rape one of his own daughters. In disbelieving the claimant's allegation, the panel notes that when the panel asked the claimant if the rape against her daughter was reported she replied that her daughter and her daughter's husband personally reported it. Asked if her daughter's husband did anything more than simply report his wife's rape, she replied that her daughter did not want to bother her with the facts and that she believed that the court proceeding about the sexual assault is ongoing. Then, she added "I think it is too private because the police will not do anything". Given that the claimant just testified that she believed that the court case in the matter was proceeding, but now she said that the police will not do anything about it, she was asked to explain the contradiction. She replied that since 2010 she had not made contact with them because they were very angry and she didn't know if they reported it.
[25] The panel finds her explanation unreasonable which undermines the claimant's credibility that her daughter was raped by her ex-husband. She had just testified that her daughter reported the sexual assault. In addition, her ex-husband was sentenced to 75 days in prison. The court document for the XXXX XXXX XXXX 2011 incident stated that the claimant's ex-husband's sentence for the XXXX XXXX XXXX 2010 incident became effective on XXXX XXXX, 2010.36 She testified that the alleged sexual assault occurred in XXXX 2010 at the claimant's home. She also testified that her ex-husband was in jail for only 15 days. Even if that was the case (and the panel does not believe it because the court document said he was sentenced to 75 days), he was ordered removed from his home for three months. Even if the claimant's ex-husband was released in XXXX 2010 or early XXXX 2010 it would be sometime in XXXX or XXXX 2010 when the restraining order would expire and there is no persuasive evidence that he breached his restraining order. The claimant testified that her ex-husband would come home as soon as the restraining order expired. Given the credibility problems with the claimant the panel does not believe that her ex-husband attempted to rape his daughter, XXXX. The panel believes that the claimant was physically assaulted twice, which the authorities dealt with adequately, and the panel does not believe that she was ever physically assaulted before 2010 by her husband.
[26] The panel has also considered the case of Sheikh which states that "... even without disbelieving every word [a claimant] has uttered, a...panel may reasonably find him so lacking in credibility that it concludes there is no credible evidence relevant to his claim... In other words, a general finding of a lack of credibility on the part of the [claimant] may conceivably extend to all relevant evidence emanating from his testimony".37
[27] The documentary evidence in regards to domestic violence is mixed however the panel finds that Croatia demonstrates that in regards to the documentary evidence, violence against women, including spousal abuse, remained a problem. In 2011, 11 women were killed as a result of domestic violence, representing 22 percent of all murders in the country. The ombudswoman for gender equality reported that courts imposed only 50 percent of available protective measures, such as psychosocial treatment and restraining orders for abusers. Her office praised police for assisting victims; however, she noted that support such as safe houses, vocational training, and financial stipends for victims of domestic violence was limited. Although the government financed most services, non-governmental organizations (NGOs) oversaw shelters and claimed that funding was insufficient and irregular. NGOs and local governments operated 17 shelters. Hotlines, counseling, and legal assistance were available to domestic violence victims. Another document38 indicates that:
"In 2003, the Croatian government adopted the Law on Protection against Domestic Violence (LPDV). By passing one of the first domestic violence laws in the region, the Croatian government has not only demonstrated its commitment to combating domestic violence but also presented itself as a leader in this effort. The adoption of the LPDV represented a significant step toward protecting domestic violence victims and holding offenders accountable. Notably, it provides both urgent (emergency) and long-term protective measures focused on victim safety, including eviction, stalking and harassment measures, restraining orders, and confiscation of weapons. It also provides two measures directed at offenders' behavior, including psychosocial treatment and addiction treatment. While a victim may, on her own, apply for protective measures under the law, most often, police file for these measures on behalf of victims. The law's jurisdiction lies in the misdemeanor system, whose judges decide whether to issue the protective measures and for what term, as well as impose a jail sentence or fine on the offender. A violation of the protective measure is punishable by a fine or imprisonment of at least ten days. The Croatian government has made evident strides in its efforts to prevent, respond to and punish domestic violence since its initial adoption of the LPDV. The government has amended the LDPV (2009) and promulgated regulations to further implementation of this law, including the Rules of Procedure in Cases of Family Violence and the National Strategy of Protection against Family Violence. The government has also issued protocols for specific sectors, such as the Ministry of Internal Affairs' Regulations on Implementation of Protective Measures for the LPDV. At the urging of women's NGOs, the Croatian government amended the Misdemeanor Law to provide temporary precautionary measures for the duration of LPDV proceedings, the violation of which is punishable by fine. While the adoption of these laws and policy documents are a positive step, there are serious problems with their implementation that negatively impact victim safety and offender accountability in Croatia."
[28] An article within the documentary evidence39 outlines some of the assistance available to women:
"In recent years civil society organisations have become very involved and have greatly contributed to the fight against domestic violence. A survey conducted in 2010 by Women's Room NGO shows that there are 32 organisations in the country actively providing support and assistance to victims of violence. Of those, 12 offer shelter services, 18 offer counselling services, one works as a centre for victims of sexual violence, and one offers counselling over the phone."
[29] Since refugee protection is forward looking, based on the documentary evidence, the panel is not persuaded that, at this time, state agencies in Croatia are not making serious efforts to provide adequate protection to people like the claimant. Despite the mixed information in the documentary evidence, the panel finds that the Croatian authorities have taken steps to address domestic violence issues.
[30] The panel finds that Croatian authorities have addressed and appear to continue to address domestic abuse within Croatian society. The preponderance of evidence also shows that, while problems still exist, serious efforts are being made to implement the laws protecting women. The panel finds that the information on Croatia though mixed provides that although not perfect there is adequate state protection at the operational level for its citizens.
Roma
[31] Documentary evidence indicates that there an estimated 30,000 and 40,000 Roma in Croatia and that while constitutional protections against discrimination applied to all minorities, open discrimination and harassment, including societal violence, continued against ethnic Serbs and Roma.40 Negative attitudes of much of the majority population are a serious obstacle to sustainable return and inclusion of minorities in all aspects of society.41
[32] While some are critical stating that efforts are insufficient, the state is making serious efforts and there are signs of progress. Ethnic minority groups including Serbs and Roma among others are guaranteed a minimum of eight representatives in the parliament and there are a number of programs to address Roma exclusion.42 The Constitutional Law on National Minorities in 2002 has provided a generally good legal framework for the protection of minorities; the implementation, however, has been patchy, often due to lacking political will.43
[33] It is estimated that only 6.5 percent of Roma had permanent jobs, while the government estimated more than 90 percent of registered Roma lived on social aid.44 While this is discouraging it also shows that the state is not willing to abandon the Roma. Government spending on programs for Roma increased from 17 million kunas ($3 million) in 2008 to 27 million kunas in 2009 ($4.8 million). The situation of minorities in Croatia has improved in the last 10 years, and while members of minorities are still confronted by harassment and discrimination, the overall ethnic and religious relations remained stable.45
[34] Documentary evidence indicates that education is free and compulsory through grade eight.46 The documentary evidence supports the claimant's allegations of mistreatment at school. Romani children faced serious obstacles in continuing their education, including discrimination in schools and a lack of family support. The number of Romani children enrolled in preschool education for the 2009-10 school year was 588, a slight decrease from 595 in 2008. However, according to the Ministry of Science, Education, and Sports, the number of new Romani students increased to 4,186 from 3,940 in 2009.
[35] The state continued and expanded reimbursement of kindergarten fees to Romani parents, introduced in the previous year, to include preschool education; thereby assisting 660 children in 49 schools and kindergartens. In addition, there were 350 scholarships to Romani students in high school, an increase of 50 compared with the previous school year. The number of Romani students receiving scholarships for university-level studies increased to 26 from 20 in the previous school year.
[36] It is clear that the state is aware of the obstacles faced by Roma students and have taken steps to help. The growth of the programs would also indicate that the programs are reaching more Roma students; a sign of some success. This success is somewhat offset by the ongoing discrimination that sees Roma students disproportionately attending Roma only classes where content is less than regular classes under the pretense that their Croatian language abilities were insufficient. The ECHR Grand Chamber ruled that the state had discriminated against 13 Romani students from Medjimurje for placement in such classes.47 It is however worth noting that a previous ruling by the same court had cleared the state from any wrongdoing and this was overturned on appeal. The flip-flop of the ECHR gives some indication that the issues around Roma only classes are not always clear cut discrimination, much less persecution. Nonetheless, just a few months after the ruling the state amended the Law on Primary and Secondary Education to provide additional language classes to children who do not speak Croatian.
[37] However, there are complaints that there is a slow or ineffective reaction by authorities to discrimination, intimidation or violence towards members of minorities.48
[38] On September 17, approximately 50 ethnic Croat parents prevented 44 Romani children from attending class in Gornji Hrascan, claiming that the school was already overcrowded and that Romani children had poor personal hygiene. The Romani children had been moved to Gornji Hrascan to ease overcrowding at a school in the nearby community of Macinec. Police intervened to prevent the situation from escalating, and local officials brokered a compromise to allow the Romani preschoolers to attend classes in Gornji Hrascan. President Josipovic stated that the effort to prevent ethnic Romani children from attending the preschool was particularly unacceptable and discriminatory.49
[39] On July 10, parliament enacted a new ombudsman law to strengthen the institution's ability to protect human rights. The law calls for the Office of the Ombudsman to merge with an NGO, the Center for Human Rights, to more effectively promote human rights and coordinate with civil society and the other specialized and independent ombudspersons. By year's end, however, the merger did not take place as the government sought to first appoint a new ombudsperson to replace retiring ombudsman Jurica Malcic. Under the law, the ombudsman is authorized to initiate shortened procedures in cases where he has established sufficient evidence that constitutional and legal rights have been violated. The ombudsman now has the authority to initiate disciplinary proceedings against institutions that fail to respond to his interventions. The ombudsman did not exercise this authority during the year.50
[40] Croatia is a functioning democracy, although needing improvement in some areas. There are free and fair elections that are in accordance with international standards; security forces reported to civilian authorities; there were no reports the government or its agents committed arbitrary or unlawful killings; there were no reports of politically motivated disappearances; the country has an ombudsman for human rights; and, individuals may freely criticize the government publicly or privately without reprisal.51
[41] However, the same documentary evidence indicates that while the law provides criminal penalties for official corruption the government did not always implement the laws effectively, and corruption remained a serious problem. Also, there are large backlogs of unresolved criminal and civil cases, ineffective prosecution of some domestic war crimes trials, a lack of progress in restoring nationalized property to the Serb Orthodox Church and Coordination of Jewish Communities in Croatia, and incidents of societal violence and discrimination against ethnic minorities and due to sexual orientation.
[42] Nonetheless, Croatia is not in a state of collapse. Croatia has a structured and functioning police service. The national police have primary responsibility for national security; in times of disorder, the prime minister and the president may call upon the military to provide security. Police obtained arrest warrants by presenting probable cause to an investigative magistrate. The police have 24 hours to justify an arrest to a magistrate. Police must provide those arrested with access to an attorney of their choice within 24 hours. The constitution and law provide for an independent judiciary, and the government generally respected judicial independence. As of December 31, 2011, prisons and youth detention facilities held 5,084 offenders, although the system's capacity was 3,921 prisoners.52 This indicates that the police service will investigate crime, even crime against minorities, although as noted above, prosecutions of race crimes are not always prosecuted. The protection of the state is not perfect.
[43] The claimants are Roma and as victims of domestic violence, when they approached the authorities for help, they were given help. There is no persuasive evidence before this panel that they would not receive help in the future should they require it. Thus, the panel finds that the claimant has not provided the requisite clear and convincing evidence that, on a balance of probabilities, state protection in Croatia is inadequate. As such, the panel finds that if the claimants return to Croatia, they could reasonably expect the state to be forthcoming with serious efforts at adequate protection. The documentary evidence with respect to protection for the Roma in Croatia is mixed but the claimants have not provided clear and convincing evidence of a failure of state protection.
[44] Considering all the documentary evidence and the claimant's testimony, the panel finds that the claimant has not discharged the burden of proving with clear and convincing evidence that state protection in Croatia would not be available to her if she were to return to Croatia and face any issues with her former spouse. While the country documentation does support that domestic violence is still a problem in Croatia, the panel finds that the claimant received state protection while she was in Croatia even if it was not perfect.
[45] The panel finds that claimant has not rebutted the presumption of adequate state protection. It is not for the panel to set out clear and convincing evidence to support the presumption of state protection, it is the responsibility of the claimant to provide such evidence to rebut the presumption. The panel finds that there is adequate state protection available to the claimant in Croatia.
CONCLUSION
[46] Based on the totality of the evidence, the panel finds that, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant has failed to rebut the presumption of state protection with clear and convincing evidence and, as the issue of state protection is applicable to both sections 96 and 97 of the IRPA, her claim must fail. Similarly, the panel finds the other claimants whose claim also rests on her claim have failed as well.
[47] The panel concludes that the claimant is not a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection and rejects her claim.
(signed)
" Edward C. Robinson "
Edward C. Robinson
May 20, 2015
